coredata.dc:title: Sustainable carbon quantum dots from Vitex negundo leaves as a synergistic nanoplatform for triple object sensing and anticounterfeiting applications
coredata.dc:creator: Korah, B.K.
Mathew, B.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are regarded as a 21st-century blessing due to the multiple applications that they contribute to society. The amazing characteristics of CDs have made them so ubiquitous around the world. Using a hydrothermal approach, we attempted to synthesize novel CDs from Vitex negundo leaves. After extensive characterization and analysis, it was found that the CDs developed not only have the basic characteristics of CDs, such as fluorescence, spherical shape with a diameter less than 10 nm, but are also highly stable under a variety of ionic concentrations, continuous photoirradiation, and long-term storage. The potential of the proposed sensor is spread over four environmental realms. The sensor tackled the first issue by detecting sunset yellow using an IFE-based fluorescence quenching technique with a LOD of 82.05 nM. The second is manganese ion sensing with a LOD of 1.25 nM using an electrochemical technique. The practical viability analysis and comparison to prior reported ones of both sensors has been found to be very effective and reasonable. Third, the ability of CDs to function as a pH sensor is explored and proven successful. The fourth potential is to shine as an anticounterfeiting agent that is both environmentally benign and safe. This work meticulously scrutinizes all four applications of synthesized CDs. The individuality of the work can be found in a variety of contexts. It is conspicuous in the precursor, manifold applications, sensitivity, evaluation and green procurement strategy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Sunset yellow
Manganese ions
Fluorescence sensing
Electrochemical sensing
pH sensor
Fluorescent ink
Green synthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence based detection of sunset yellow
Mechanism for the fluorescence sensing of sunset yellow
Fluorescence analysis of SY in food samples
Electrochemical based detection of manganese ions
Electroanalytical performance towards manganese ion detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Thanks to Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, Kerala for providing University Junior Research Fellowship and DST SAIF for providing the instrumentation facility for Raman and lifetime analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption spectrum, emission spectrum and excitation spectrum of CDs, (b) fluorescence spectra at different excitation wavelengths, and (c) FT-IR spectrum of CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, HR XPS spectra corresponding to (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s of CDs.
(a) TEM image of CDs, (b) HR-TEM image of CDs, (c) size distribution histogram of CDs.
(a) Raman spectrum, (b) XRD spectrum, (c) Zeta potential curve, and (d) DLS spectrum of CDs.
Stability of CDs at various (a) ionic concentrations, (b) photoirradiation time, (c) storage time, (d) temperature, and (e) ionic solutions.

(a) Fluorescence spectrum of CDs at different concentrations of sunset yellow, (b) dependence of F0/F on the concentration of sunset yellow, and (c) interference study of CDs-SY system in the presence of (1) tartrazine, (2) alizarin red, (3) methyl thymol blue, (4) glutathione, (5) acid fuchsin, (6) phenylalanine, (7) leucine, (8) arginine, (9) valine, and (10) glucose.

(a) Fluorescence spectrum of CDs at various pH settings (3–10), (b) linear dependance of intensity on pH, (c) Intensity values on cyclical shifting of CDs over alternate pH conditions (pH = 3 and pH = 10).
(a) CV runs of bare electrode, CDs modified electrode, and in the presence of Mn(II) in PBS, (b) EIS spectra of CDs/GCE and bare GCE, (c) effect of storage, and (d) effect of pH.

(a) Selective detection of Mn(II) ions, (b) effect of scan rate on CDs/GCE/Mn(II), (c) Differential pulse voltammogram of CDs/GCE with Mn(II) concentrations (10–50 nM); and (d) linear plot of concentration of Mn(II) against peak current.
Filter paper drawings and letters produced with CDs-based fluorescent ink under UV irradiation.
Illustration of synthesis strategy of CDs from Vitex negundo leaves.
Electron transfer mechanism for the sensing of Mn(II) ions using carbon dot modified glassy carbon electrode (CDs/GCE).
Comparison of reported techniques for the detection of sunset yellow.
Real sample analysis of SY detection using CDs.
Comparative analysis of different techniques for the electrochemical detection of Mn(II) ions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Real sample analysis of Mn(II) detection using CDs.

head.ce:title: Sustainable carbon quantum dots from Vitex negundo leaves as a synergistic nanoplatform for triple object sensing and anticounterfeiting applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Priyadarsini Hills P. O, Kottayam, 686 560, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Priyadarsini Hills P. O, Kottayam, 686 560, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are regarded as a 21st-century blessing due to the multiple applications that they contribute to society. The amazing characteristics of CDs have made them so ubiquitous around the world. Using a hydrothermal approach, we attempted to synthesize novel CDs from Vitex negundo leaves. After extensive characterization and analysis, it was found that the CDs developed not only have the basic characteristics of CDs, such as fluorescence, spherical shape with a diameter less than 10 nm, but are also highly stable under a variety of ionic concentrations, continuous photoirradiation, and long-term storage. The potential of the proposed sensor is spread over four environmental realms. The sensor tackled the first issue by detecting sunset yellow using an IFE-based fluorescence quenching technique with a LOD of 82.05 nM. The second is manganese ion sensing with a LOD of 1.25 nM using an electrochemical technique. The practical viability analysis and comparison to prior reported ones of both sensors has been found to be very effective and reasonable. Third, the ability of CDs to function as a pH sensor is explored and proven successful. The fourth potential is to shine as an anticounterfeiting agent that is both environmentally benign and safe. This work meticulously scrutinizes all four applications of synthesized CDs. The individuality of the work can be found in a variety of contexts. It is conspicuous in the precursor, manifold applications, sensitivity, evaluation and green procurement strategy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data is available as electronic supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology has become one of the foremost themes in a multitude of areas since the turn of the century, including environmental protection, electronics manufacturing, biomedical applications because of its outstanding features. A novel family of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots, has supplanted the classic semi-quantum dots. Carbon dots (CDs) have received a lot of attention in recent years due to their ease of manufacture, bio-compatibility, and a wide range of applications [1–6]. Carbon dots were originally introduced in 2004 during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes. Carbon dots are tiny, quasi-spherical particles having a diameter of less than 10 nm. The advantages of carbon dots, such as carbon source accessibility, inexpensiveness, and outstanding fluorescence characteristics, have sparked a lot of interest in these materials. In comparison to typical fluorescent organic dyes and semiconducting quantum dots, carbon dots are resistant to chemicals, nontoxic, and incredibly resistant to photo corrosion [7,8,69,70].

Sunset yellow (SY) is a yellow-colored artificial azo dye made from the disodium salt of 2-Hydroxy-1- (4-sulfonato phenyl azo naphthalene-6-sulfonate). Beverages, creamy desserts, pharmaceuticals, patisserie, and a variety of goods all contain it [9]. According to WHO recommendations [10,11], the daily permissible consumption of sunset yellow is less than 2.5 mg/kg. This dye is toxic above this limit, causing a serious hazard over the world, causing failure symptoms of gastric, immunological, and neurological organs, especially among children [12,13]. The entire regular consumption of sunset yellow food dye must be kept under control so that the dye's daily acceptable intake (ADI) is not exceeded. As a consequence, appropriate measures for determining sunset yellow dye must be developed in order to maintain sustainable quality. Various supramolecular and composite materials have been developed by the researches all over the world to tackle the issue [14–18]. Plenty of analytical techniques have already been used to determine the levels of food dyes in various food materials [19–23]. However, they are ineffectual, due to the time-consuming sample processing and sophisticated equipment required by all of the mentioned approaches. As a result, there is a pressing need for the creation of a method that involves simple sample preparation and precise equipment. Fluorescence spectroscopy has now been recognized as a crucial optical technology capable of providing a versatile, accurate, and simple detection approach amongst all. During the last few years, a great deal of work has gone into developing an effective fluorescence detector for sunset yellow recognition [24–26].

Manganese is the earth's third most common transition metal [27]. It is a necessary trace element for human well-being [28]. It is involved in the activation of several enzymes involved in human, animal, and plant metabolic processes [28]. Manganese is used in a wide range of products, including battery, fertilizer, ceramic, and electric coil. Manganese, on the other hand, is a necessary element for a range of species [29], but excessive amounts can create issues in the neurological and respiratory systems [30]. As a result, monitoring and determining manganese levels in ecological resources is essential. Many analytical methods have been used to detect manganese, including atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [31], coprecipitation [32,33], solid phase extraction (SPE) [34–36], and electrochemistry [37–39]. Electrochemical approaches have gotten a lot of interest because of their speed, efficiency, simplicity, and responsiveness.

In addition, pH has a vital function in a variety of disciplines, ranging from ecological to commercial to biological systems. As a result, developing better pH sensing is becoming a popular subject. Several materials for pH sensing have been developed, including Ag@SiO2 core-shell particles, QDs, encoded red fluorescent protein detector, and semiconducting polymer dots, according to some research [40–42]. But there is a lack of an environment friendly sensor of pH monitoring. Carbon dot based fluorescent ink is yet another topic of the hour which have employed a lot nowadays.

In this work, we synthesized CDs from Vitex negundo leaves using a simple hydrothermal method. Vitex negundo, commonly known as the Chinese chaste tree or five-leaved chaste tree, or horseshoe vitex is indeed popular in folk medicine, especially in South and Southeast Asia. Casticin, isoorientin, luteolin, and D fructose are the main components in leaf juice [43,44]. It is thought to have medical qualities such as antipyretic, anti-inflammatory, and anti-arthritis. CDs obtained from this green source was characterized to obtain new knowledge on the material properties. It was employed for four major sectors. Using fluorescence spectroscopy, CDs were able to successfully detect, the commonly used food color, sunset yellow with an excellent LOD. Food sample analysis was also done to ensure its practical viability. It was also employed as a pH sensor due to its linear variation in intensity under different pH conditions. Through electrochemical method, the synthesized CDs detected Mn(II) ions with a detection limit of 1.25 nM. It is important to note that there are negligible reports in the electrochemical sensing of Mn(II) ions using green synthesized CDs. Most of the works in literature either employs chemical substances or modifications for the detection applications. In addition to this, CDs were applied as a fluorescent ink for drawing and writing applications. This work is noteworthy in that it tackles a myriad of issues. This is the first report on CDs made from Vitex negundo leaves. As a result, we were able to make new CDs and define it through thorough scrutiny. Thus, it adds to our understanding of material properties by supplying extra data and information. There are separate literature reports on the use of CDs for SY and Mn(II) sensing. pH sensors based on CDs have also been produced, as well as fluorescent ink. The main distinction between our work and that of others is that we use the same CDs to effectively accomplish all four applications without the need for any extra components and with high selectivity and sensitivity. Secondly, the sensor developed is non-toxic, water soluble, stable and has proved to be successful in real life situations. Also, the synthesis procedure is very simple and precursor used is inexpensive. We thus developed a unique sensor for SY and Mn(II) detection, a non-toxic anticounterfeiting tool, and a biocompatible pH sensor using a simple and ecologically safe technique. In brief, the sensor made from a green source and developed utilizing a green approach has been successfully implemented to fostering a green environment.

Vitex negundo leaves were obtained from the nearby garden. Metal salts employed were lead(II) nitrate, manganese(II) chloride, ferrous sulphate, copper(II) chloride, mercury(II) chloride, potassium dichromate, zinc sulphate, nickel(II) chloride, cadmium chloride and cobalt chloride. Dyes such as sunset yellow, tartrazine, alizarin red, methyl thymol blue, acid fuchsin, and amino acids including arginine, leucine, valine, and phenylalanine were employed. Others used comprise glutathione, glucose, sodium chloride, disodium hydrogen phosphate, sodium dihydrogen phosphate and quinine sulphate. Double distilled water was used during the experimentation.

FT-IR spectra were taken using a Perkin Elmer-400 spectrometer. UV–vis. spectrum assessment was conducted with a Shimadzu UV-2450, Japan. To produce the photoluminescence spectra, a Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrofluorometer was adopted. A X'PERT-PRO X-ray spectrometer has been used to obtain the XRD. JEOL JEM-2100 microscope was employed to obtain HR-TEM images. The DLS and zeta were done using a Horiba SZ-100 scientific nanoparticle analyzer. A Thermo Scientific ESCALAB spectrometer was used to undertake the XPS investigation. All electrochemical experiments have been performed using a three-electrode arrangement on a Biologic SP 200 workstation. The working electrode was a glassy carbon electrode (GCE), with the reference electrode being an Ag/AgCl electrode and the counter electrode being a Pt wire.

Carbon dots were synthesized from Vitex negundo leaves using hydrothermal method. Scheme 1 depicts synthesis procedure for carbon dots. Hydrothermal carbonization of Vitex negundo leaves extract resulted in the formation of brown colored solution. The exact procedure is as follows; 5 g of Vitex negundo leaves were weighed and dispersed in 150 ml of water. After hydrothermal carbonization for 7 h at 200 °C, a brown-colored solution was obtained. Optimization studies are included in Fig. S1. It was centrifuged and filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane. The obtained blue fluorescent CDs were stored at low temperature.

Fluorescence spectroscopy was employed for the detection of sunset yellow. It was done by measuring the fluorescence intensities of carbon dot solutions containing different concentrations of sunset yellow (0–20 μM). For selectivity studies, 0.001 M concentrations of various amino acids and food colors were prepared and fluorescence intensities were recorded. Interference studies were conducted by adding 0.001 M concentrations of various interfering substances to the carbon dot solution containing the same concentration of sunset yellow. The fluorescence intensities were recorded in each case and analyzed. Ionic stability, photostability and storage stability were also carried out using the same technique. For determining ionic stability, the fluorescence intensities of CDs dissolved in various ionic concentrations (0.2–1.0 M NaCl) were measured. Photostability study was conducted by recording the spectrum of CDs under UV light at regular intervals of time. Similarly, storage stability was conducted by recording the fluorescence spectra over a period of two weeks. Excitation wavelength was fixed to 330 nm throughout the experiment.

Different pH solutions (3–10) were prepared in sodium phosphate buffer solution using disodium hydrogen phosphate and sodium dihydrogen phosphate. Carbon dots were dissolved in the prepared pH solutions and fluorescence spectra were recorded. Excitation wavelength was 330 nm and experiment were carried out under room temperature.

A glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was used as the working electrode in electrochemical experiments. The solution containing CDs was applied to the GCE surface and allowed to dry. The cyclic voltammograms of bare GCE, CD-coated GCE (CD/GCE), and CD/GCE in the presence of various metal ions were all recorded. Different quantities of Mn(II) ions (10–50 nM) were prepared to obtain differential pulse voltammetry data. At various scan speeds, the cyclic voltammograms of the modified electrode in the presence of Mn(II) ions were studied. In addition, the EIS of bare GCE and modified GCE were studied.

Real sample analysis for the detection of sunset yellow through fluorescence method and sensing of Mn(II) via electrochemical method were conducted. For the fluorescence sensing of sunset yellow, samples included were soft drinks, sauce, tap water, and river water. Test materials, soft drink and sauce were collected from local market and mixed with water before being heated to 70 °C. This was filtered and centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 15 min) before dilution for the analysis. Tap water and river water were filtered and centrifuged before use. No additional pretreatment was employed for the water samples. The fluorescence spectra were recorded for each sample spiked with different concentrations of sunset yellow. In the case of electrochemical sensing of Mn(II) ions, current values were noted for the water samples spiked with various Mn(II) concentrations via differential pulse voltammetry technique.

CDs without any chemical treatment were directly injected to the ink cartridge of the ink pen. It was used to draw and write images and words on filter paper. After sufficient drying in open air, it was placed under UV light and photographic images were taken.

The hydrothermal process was used to make carbon dots from Vitex negundo leaves. The brown colored solution formed after hydrothermal carbonization of Vitex negundo leaves extract, was characterized using various techniques. Absorption, emission and excitation spectra are shown in Fig. 1 . The peak at 328 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectra can be attributed to the presence of C=O bonds with the n-π∗ transition. Furthermore, the peak at 255 nm is due to the existence of COO− groups and the C=C, π- π∗ transition (Fig. 1a). The excitation spectra show two peaks and also it is slightly greater than the emission spectrum. The additional fluorescence excitation peak at 328 nm is near to the absorption peak at 328 nm which is a clear indication that the additional peak originated due to the emission from n-π∗ transition [45,46]. An excitation independent spectrum was obtained with the maximum emission at 450 nm when excited at 330 nm (Fig. 1b). It is in contrast to the frequently reported excitation-dependent multicolored emission of carbon dots synthesized, which typically have different emission spectra at various excitation wavelengths. The homogeneous chemical characteristics on the surface of the as-prepared CDs are responsible for this observation [47,48]. The quantum yield of the carbon dots synthesized was found to be 12.2%. The high quantum yield of the CDs is influenced by a number of factors. Here, CDs have been generated from the leaves of Vitex negundo, which are rich in casticin, isoorientin, chrysophenol D, luteolin, p–hydroxybenzoic acid, d-fructose, and numerous other essential oils [44]. The carbonization of these constituents yields CDs with several functional groups, primarily containing nitrogen and oxygen as heteroatoms. Quantum yield of manufactured CDs is influenced by the presence of heteroatoms [76]. In addition, parameters such as reaction temperature and time play a significant effect in the quantum yield and fluorescence of CDs [77]. In this preparation, the temperature and time have been optimized for maximum quantum yield (Fig. S1). The high fluorescence and QY of the CDs as prepared was the outcome of a combination of all these elements.

The surface of carbon dots is enriched with different functional groups. Analysis of FT-IR spectrum is one of the basic techniques to investigate the different functionalities. As shown in Fig. 1c, there was a peak about 3262 cm−1 that was attributed to stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H. The peak at 1634 cm−1and 1396 cm−1 indicated the carboxyl group vibrations. Stretching vibrations of C–O/C–N are produced in the region 1040 to 1500 cm−1 [3,49]. Further confirmation of the elemental composition has been done using XPS analysis. As expected, there were three distinct peaks in the survey spectrum corresponding to the presence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen (Fig. 2 ). To further validate the type of bonding, high resolution spectra corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s were analyzed. In the C1s spectrum, there were three peaks at 283.5 eV, 285.8 eV, 286.9 eV which belongs to C=C, C–N, and C=O respectively. Deconvoluted N1s spectrum clearly depicts the presence of C–N–C and N–H functionalities through the peaks at 398.8 and 401.4 eV. The information about oxygen containing bonds is obtained from the analysis of O1s spectrum. There are mainly two peaks at 530.1 and 531.6 eV corresponding to C=O and C–OH/C–O–C respectively [50]. EDX analysis also confirms the presence of carbon and oxygen as represented in Fig. S2.

Carbon dots are generally regarded as spherical shaped carbon-based nanomaterials with diameter less than 10 nm. To investigate the morphological aspects of the synthesized CDs, TEM images were obtained as shown in Fig. 3 . Well dispersed sphere-shaped carbon dots with an average diameter of 6.5 nm obtained were in agreement with the reported features of CDs. Lattice spacing of 0.22 nm corresponds to the graphitic carbon (100) plane. The average diameter obtained from TEM distribution histogram matches with the value obtained through DLS analysis (Fig. 4 d). XRD was used to investigate the crystallinity of carbon dots. The sample under investigation has a 24.28° X-ray powder diffraction pattern record. More broadness in XRD peaks resulted in lesser crystallinity, according to the Debye Scherer equation [51]. A low crystallinity is indicated by a broader peak at 2ϴ = 24.28° (Fig. 4b). Raman spectroscopy is another technique that gives more information about the structure and crystallinity of CDs. Raman spectrum, as shown in Fig. 4a has two major peaks at 1345.2 and 1581.8 cm−1 corresponding to D and G bands, respectively. The extent of graphitization correlated with the carbon dots is represented by the G-band (sp2 hybridization), whereas the degree of defects and functionalization is represented by the D-band (sp3 hybridization). The intensity ratio (IG/ID) also confirms the slight crystalline nature which was found to be 1.17. Lifetime of CDs was found to be 10 ns as depicted in Fig. S3.

Zeta potential curve obtained is depicted in Fig. 4c. Because it is a measurement of charge repulsion or attraction between particles from their surface to diffusion layer bonding, the zeta potential is a sign of nanoparticle stability. Particles with a high zeta potential self-stabilize because their charge prevents particle agglomeration and improves stability. The −19.5 mV value found indicates that carbon dots are stable. Surface functional groups contribute to the negative charge.

The stability evaluation of nanomaterial is very necessary for detection of target substances. Stability of carbon dot with different concentrations of NaCl was analyzed (Fig. 5 a). There was no notable change in fluorescence intensity, thereby confirming the ionic stability. The stability of carbon dot with different time duration of UV light irradiation was evaluated. The carbon dot was kept under light for different time intervals and it was observed that there was no change in fluorescence intensity with time (Fig. 5b). Carbon dot should be stable for longer time period. In order to ensure the storage stability, the fluorescence intensity was measured during a time period of two weeks (Fig. 5c). The observed stability upon storage clearly depicts the potential of CDs for multifaceted applications. Moreover, CDs were also stable at different temperature and in the presence of different interfering ions (Fig. 5d and e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence based detection of sunset yellow
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence based detection of pollutants using carbon dots has been a widely investigated topic [71–75]. Carbon dots were found successful for the sensing of sunset yellow through fluorescence quenching process. To examine the optimum pH and incubation time for obtaining the best result, optimization studies were conducted. The obtained optimum pH was 3 and the incubation time was 5 min as shown in Fig. S4. This means that maximum quenching was attained in the acidic pH and the reaction has reached maximum efficiency within 5 min. With increase in time there was no considerable change in the quenching intensity. Different concentrations of sunset yellow were added to the carbon dot solution to examine the change in the fluorescence intensity. There was a regular quenching in the fluorescence of CDs with increase in concentration of sunset yellow (Fig. 6 a). CDs detected SY within a linear range of 2–20 μM with an LOD of 82.05 nM. The linear plot obtained is shown in Fig. 6b. The selectivity of CDs over other food additives and amino acids towards the detection of SY is depicted in Fig. S5. Interference study was also conducted to ensure that the developed sensor could effectively work even in the presence of other foreign species in the same concentration (0.001 M) (Fig. 6c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the fluorescence sensing of sunset yellow
ce:section.ce:para: Exploration of the mechanism was possible only after further studies and analysis. Therefore, the UV–vis spectrum of SY and emission spectrum of CDs were compared. A good spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of SY and the emission spectrum of CDs demonstrated the viability of IFE or FRET as a potential quenching mechanism (Fig. S6a). The spectrum overlapping indicates that the analyzer may be able to absorb CDs emissions [52]. An accurate conclusion could only be made after assessing the lifetimes of CDs and CDs-SY complexes. FRET is associated with a reduction in lifetime, whereas IFE does not [53,54]. As shown in Fig. S6b, the lifetime curve of CDs and CDs-SY systems were essentially identical. This established the inner filter effect as the mechanism. Compared to other works in literature the synthesized CDs could detect SY with good limit of detection (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence analysis of SY in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical use of the sensor is possible only if it is found successful in real sample detection. To investigate the practical viability of the sample, real samples including tap water, river water, soft drink and sauce were selected. After suitable pre-treatment, the real sample solutions spiked with different concentrations of SY were prepared. The fluorescence intensity of each sample solutions was recorded and analyzed carefully. The recovery rates and RSD values obtained suggests the potential of the developed environment friendly sensor for application in real samples (Table 2 ).

Additional application as a pH sensor stemmed from the assessment of the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the produced CDs. Different buffer solutions (phosphate buffer) were prepared to evaluate the stability of CDs under various pH values. The fluorescence intensity values of CDs dissolved in varying pH solutions (3–10) were recorded. Interestingly, raising the pH resulted in a linear decrease in fluorescence (Fig. 7 a). A straight-line graph was obtained when the fluorescence intensity values were plotted against the corresponding pH values (Fig. 7b). Furthermore, fluorescence intensity of CDs was reversible across a pH range of 3–10 throughout uninterrupted six cycles of acidification and alkalization (Fig. 7c). The dynamic protonation/deprotonation of synthesized carbon dots in alkaline and acidic environments deemed the likely explanation underlying pH-sensitive fluorescence. The high vibrational coupling of hydroxyl functionalities generated by hydrogen bonding can cause energy level widening and enhance the structural stiffness of CDs at low pH levels. As a result, fluorescent intensity of carbon dots was high at acidic environment. However, the absence of the H-bonding interaction during deprotonation in alkaline environment may result in lower vibrational couplings and more distinct energy levels, resulting in a reduction in intensity [59,60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical based detection of manganese ions
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical detection of metal ions using green synthesized carbon quantum dots is a less explored topic. For a carbon dot to be applied as an electrochemical sensor, it should have some superior properties and activity more than the bare electrodes. Therefore, the electrochemical capabilities of the synthesized CDs were examined prior to the application proceedings. Cyclic voltammogram of the carbon dot modified glassy carbon electrode and bare electrode was recorded and analyzed. The modified one exhibits more activity than the bare one, as shown in Fig. 8 a, and there are two separate peaks in the presence of Mn(II) ions, accounting for potential detecting ability of CDs. The conclusion of the developed electrode as an electrochemical sensor is also supported by impedance spectra. Because the emergence of a smaller semicircle (lower resistance) on the modified electrode compared to the bare one reveals the higher conductance of the modified electrode (Fig. 8b). Another important property that a sensor should possess is the reproducibility. The cyclic voltammogram of the CDs-modified GCE was recorded after two months of storage and compared with the fresh one. As shown in Fig. 8c, there is negligible difference between the two, thereby ensuring the reproducibility of the sensor. With this consideration of CDs as an electrochemical sensor for the detection of Mn(II) ions, it is necessary to find the optimum conditions for achieving maximum sensitivity and selectivity. For that the sensing experiment of Mn(II) using CDs modified GCE in various pH solutions were conducted (Fig. 8d). Analysis of cyclic voltammogram corresponding to each pH, led to the selection of pH 7 as the optimum condition. Maximum current was obtained at this value rather than others. There were no notable peaks for Mn(II) detection at very lower pH, indicating that there is less chance for sensing at acidic conditions. This observation could be due to an overabundance of hydrogen ions. We can see smaller peaks at higher pH, specifically pH greater than 7, indicating that sensing is possible. However, peak current values fall under basic pH conditions, which may be due to the production of hydroxides [61]. As a result, further experiments were carried out at the optimal pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electroanalytical performance towards manganese ion detection
ce:section.ce:para: Any sort of sensing becomes fully successful, only if the sensor is able to sense the target selectively and sensitively. Thus, the selectivity of the sensor was analyzed at the optimized pH. Metal ions such as Cr(VI), Co(II), Cd(II), Ni(II), Cu(II), Fe(II), Hg(II), Pb(II), and Zn(II) were prepared and examined whether they can be sensed by the proposed sensor. As depicted in Fig. 9 a, only our target analyte, Mn(II) was able to produce two distinct peaks during the experiment. Thus, after validating the selectivity of the sensor, we moved on to check whether there is any effect on the electrochemical sensing of Mn(II) in the presence of interfering substances in the same concentration as that of the analyte (0.001 M). Negligible difference in the current values confirmed that sensor has higher sensitivity even in the presence of interfering substances (Fig. S7). The reversible nature of the sensor was investigated by recording the cyclic voltammogram of CDs-modified electrode in the presence of Mn(II) ions at various scan rates. An increase in the current values with increasing scan rate was observed as shown in Fig. 9b.

DPV analysis was employed to find the limit of detection and linear range for the determination of Mn(II) using the sensor. Different concentrations of Mn(II) ions were prepared and voltammograms were recorded using the modified electrode. The value of current increases with increase in Mn(II) ion concentration (Fig. 9c). A linear plot was drawn with the concentration against current values as illustrated in Fig. 9d. The linear equation obtained was y = 0.01846x + 3.4543, with limit of detection 1.25 nM, over a range of 10.0–50.0 nM. This was much lower than the previously reported ones in literature. A comparative analysis was done and presented in Table 3 .

The mechanism behind the electrochemical detection is yet another important matter of consideration. It is the appearance of two separate peaks that led us to the conclusion that CDs has the ability to selectively detect Mn(II) ions. The investigation of reason behind the peaks lead to the plausible mechanism behind electrochemical detection of manganese ions. The surface of carbon dots is covered with different functional groups. The interactions that existed between these functionalities and manganese ions, led to the adsorption of ions on modified electrode surface. This specific interaction is considered to be rooted from the plant source from which we have prepared the CDs. The chemical components in the plant source does play a crucial role in the determination of surface functionalities [62–65]. Ion exchanges between protons upon the surfaces of CDs and manganese ions is feasible after adsorption. Following that, Mn(II) ions are reduced and oxidized by an electron transfer mechanism, as seen by the oxidation and reduction peaks in Fig. 8a. A schematic diagram is also included to get a clear idea about the electrochemical mechanism (Scheme 2 ). Below is an illustration of a possible electron transfer mechanism.
Mn2+ + CDs-2COOH → CDs-2COO− -Mn2+ + 2H+Ion exchange
CDs-2COO−-Mn2+ + 2e− + 2H+→ Mn(0)GCE surface + CDs-2COOHReduction
Mn(0)GCE surface → Mn2+ + 2e−Oxidation

After examining the electrochemical ability, reproducibility and performance of the sensor, one thing was made sure that the CDs can be applied in real life without any fear of environmental toxicity. As an example, tap water and river water were collected. After pre-treatment, they were spiked with Mn(II) ions of different concentrations. Current values were recorded, recovery percentages and RSD values were calculated and listed in Table 4 . RSD were below 2% and the recovery percentages were also satisfactory. Thus, the ability of the sensor to be applied as a practical probe for environmental protection applications is confirmed.

In today's society, there are a slew of issues including product counterfeiting. There are a variety of items that can be simply recreated using basic techniques while maintaining accuracy and precision. Even currency counterfeiting is a major issue in the global economy. Duplication of electrical gadgets, packaged foods, vehicle commodities, currencies, and other areas could be impacted as a result of this. To combat counterfeiting, a variety of anti-counterfeiting solutions are accessible in the market. The as synthesized CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence under UV light. This fluorescence property was explored for the application as a fluorescent ink. Handwritten words and drawings using the pen on filter paper was easily visible under UV light when carbon dot solution was directly pumped into it without any chemical treatment (Fig. 10 ). Under ambient light, the CDs based fluorescent ink is colorless, but under UV light, they appear as bright blue and flow freely while writing, with no leaking or agglomeration within the pen. Furthermore, the results is clear, durable, pollution-free, ecological, and readily removable. As a result, CDs ink could be a viable replacement for fluorescent pens in anticounterfeiting applications.

To conclude, novel CDs were prepared from Vitex negundo leaves for the first time using hydrothermal method. The new material thus synthesized was characterized thoroughly for investigating the optical and morphological properties. Sphere-shaped CDs with an average diameter of 6.5 nm with good quantum yield, water solubility exhibited bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation. CDs were found applicable in four diverse areas; (i) CDs were able to detect sunset yellow in food samples with an LOD of 82.05 nM within a linear range of 2–20 μM through an IFE based fluorescence quenching process, (ii) a linear decrease in the intensity of CDs with increasing pH led to the application of CDs as pH sensor, (iii) CDs detected Mn(II) ions via an electrochemical with an excellent LOD of 1.25 nM within the detection range 10–50 nM, and (iv) Finally, exploration of the fluorescence property of CDs led to the development of CDs-based fluorescent ink for anticounterfeiting applications. Thus, through a one-step hydrothermal method we synthesized CDs which was found to be successful for multifaceted applications. The main highlight of the proposed sensor is that it performs all the applications to ensure sustainability without posing threat to the environment. A product obtained out of a green source is thus utilized to keep the planet green. We attempted to demonstrate that the CDs synthesized has the amazing potential in the proposed applications through this work. More research is needed to make this sensor functional so that it can be leveraged to its full potential. Real-world applicability of the sensor requires additional experimental and theoretical study. This work portrays how a CD created from an environmentally friendly source can be used for a variety of purposes without having a negative impact on the surrounding natural environment.
Binila K Korah: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing- Original draft preparation.
Beena Mathew: Supervision, Validation, Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thanks to Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, Kerala for providing University Junior Research Fellowship and DST SAIF for providing the instrumentation facility for Raman and lifetime analysis.

ce:section.ce:para:
